title: Thrown stoneware plate
subtitle: A wide flat plate with a low rim, thrown in smooth stoneware and glaze-fired at cone 6
excerpt: A thrown plate looks straightforward and behaves the opposite way. The wide shallow floor wants to warp during drying; the rim wants to crack as the centre dries faster than the edge. This tutorial covers a 220 mm dinner plate in smooth stoneware, taken from a centred 900 g mound through pulling, slow controlled drying, bisque firing at cone 06, dipped glaze, and a cone 6 glaze firing.
sourceNotes: Thrown plate construction derived from first principles. Cassell's The Book of the Home (1880s editions) for the historical framing of flatware in studio practice; Encyclopaedia Britannica, eleventh edition (1911), pottery entry. Orton Ceramic Foundation cone temperature reference for cone 06 (999°C) and cone 6 (1222°C).

glossaryTerms.definition: Pushing the clay mound into a state where it rotates without wobbling under the hands. The first move before opening.
The drying stage where the clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, join, and carve.
Air-dried but unfired clay. Fragile; the final stage before bisque firing.
Unfired clay in any drying stage. Loses water during the early phase of bisque firing.
The first kiln firing, around cone 06 (999°C), that converts greenware into hard, porous bisqueware ready for glazing.
A recessed ring cut into the base of the leather-hard piece that lifts it off the kiln shelf and refines the base profile.
Water-based wax emulsion brushed onto bisque to mask areas the glaze should not stick to; chiefly the foot ring.

aliases: how to throw a flat plate

projectSchedule.title: Throw the plate
projectSchedule.body: Centre 900 g, open wide, compress the floor twice, set a low rim. Wire off. Cover loosely with plastic to slow the rim drying.

projectSchedule.title: Trim the foot ring
projectSchedule.body: At leather-hard. Invert the plate on a clay collar or foam pad and trim a foot ring at 140-160 mm diameter.

projectSchedule.title: Bone-dry check before bisque
projectSchedule.body: Plate should feel room temperature against the cheek and look uniformly pale. Any cool patch is residual moisture; wait another day.

projectSchedule.title: Bisque fire at cone 06
projectSchedule.body: Load the plate on a stilt-free shelf with the foot ring directly on kiln wash. Fire to cone 06 (999°C). Cool to room temperature inside the kiln.

projectSchedule.title: Wax the foot ring and glaze
projectSchedule.body: Brush wax resist on the foot ring. Dip the plate in cone 6 glaze; touch up the finger marks. Allow the glaze to dry to the touch.

projectSchedule.title: Glaze fire at cone 6
projectSchedule.body: Load the plate flat on a kiln-washed shelf. Fire to cone 6 (1222°C). Cool with the lid closed until below 80°C before opening.

content.text: A plate is the most demanding flat form on the wheel. The wide floor dries unevenly (centre to edge) and warps; the rim cracks if it dries faster than the body; the foot ring needs trimming carefully because the inverted plate sits on its own rim during trimming. This tutorial works in 900 g of smooth stoneware to make a 220 mm dinner plate with a low 15 mm rim, finished at cone 6 with a commercial pre-mixed mid-fire glaze. A potter's wheel and access to a kiln firing to cone 6 are both required.

attrs.title: Silica + kiln safety
attrs.body: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard and silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only. Greenware sanding happens outdoors with a mask and never in a kitchen-air-shared space. For the kiln firing: ensure the kiln area is ventilated, either by a downdraft vent or by an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C; thermal shock cracks the work and any reduction firing leaves carbon monoxide inside the chamber. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio area.

items.name: Liner brush
items.substitution: for touching up dip finger marks

items.name: Glaze bucket
items.substitution: wide enough to dip a 220 mm plate

content.text: Throwing the plate
1. Centre 900 g

Wedge 900 g of smooth stoneware and slap it firmly onto a bat attached to the wheel head. The bat matters more for a plate than for a cup: a plate cannot be lifted off the wheel by hand without distorting, so it must dry on the bat it was thrown on.
Centring
is complete when the mound feels still under resting palms.
2. Flatten the centred mound

Press the heel of one hand down on the top of the centred mound and push it flat, spreading clay outward. The mound should end up about 30 mm tall and 150 mm wide. The flattened mound is now the rough footprint of the plate; the floor of the plate will come from this disc and the rim will rise from the outer edge.
3. Open out to the floor diameter

Press both thumbs into the centre of the flattened mound and pull outward to a floor diameter of 180-190 mm. Leave 8-10 mm of clay between the bat and the floor; this becomes the thick base that supports the foot ring trim later. Compress the floor with the pads of the fingers swept in a slow spiral from centre out to the wall.
4. Compress the floor a second time with a wooden rib

A plate cracks at the floor more than any other thrown form because the wide expanse of clay shrinks unevenly during drying. Compress the floor a second time with the flat of a wooden rib pressed firmly downward and swept slowly from centre to edge. The rib aligns the clay particles parallel to the floor, which is the orientation that resists cracking as the floor dries.
5. Pull the rim wall and shape

The remaining ring of clay at the outer edge becomes the rim. Pull this ring upward in one pass to about 20 mm height, knuckle on the outside, finger pad inside. Then shape: tilt the rim outward at roughly 30 degrees from vertical with the inside finger pressing outward as the outside hand supports. Final rim height should be 12-15 mm above the floor and angled gently outward; a plate rim, not a bowl wall.

Trim the rim level with the needle tool, then compress with a damp chamois folded over the edge. Wire off the base, leaving the plate sitting on its bat. Do not attempt to lift the plate off the bat; flat forms slump and distort if handled wet.
Drying slowly and evenly

Lift the bat off the wheel and set on a board in a draught-free spot. Cover the rim loosely with a strip of plastic, leaving the centre exposed. The centre wants to dry slower than the edge but the edge is exposed to more airflow; the plastic at the rim slows it down to match. Check at 8 hours; if the rim feels even slightly tackier than the floor, leave the plastic on for another 8 hours.
The plate reaches
leather-hard
in 24-48 hours depending on room humidity. At this stage the surface feels cool but not damp and a fingernail leaves a clean mark without pulling clay away.
Trimming the foot ring

Invert the leather-hard plate carefully onto a foam pad or a thrown clay collar centred on the wheel head. The plate now sits on its rim; which is why the rim must have been compressed and not too thin. Centre the plate by tapping the edge gently while spinning the wheel slowly; the plate is centred when the edges stop wobbling visibly.
Mark a
foot ring

circle at 140-160 mm diameter with the needle tool; well inside the rim line. Cut with a loop tool: an outer line first to define the foot ring, then carve away the inner area to a depth of 4-5 mm, leaving the foot ring standing 4-5 mm proud. The foot ring itself is 6-8 mm wide. Bevel the inside and outside edges of the ring; sharp edges chip in handling.
Drying to bone-dry
Turn the plate right-way-up onto a board. Leave uncovered for four to seven days to reach
bone-dry

. Test by pressing the inside of the wrist against the floor of the plate: bone-dry clay feels room temperature, damp clay feels cool. Wait until every part of the plate reads the same temperature before bisque loading.
Bisque firing
Load the plate on a kiln-washed shelf with the foot ring directly on the shelf.
Greenware
plates can share shelves; rims can touch, but do not stack a second plate inside a first because the lower plate may slump as it loses water.
Bisque fire
to cone 06 (999°C) using a slow climb through the early hours to drive off residual moisture. Cool inside the kiln to room temperature before unloading.
Glazing
Wipe the bisque plate with a damp sponge to remove kiln dust. Apply
wax resist

to the foot ring and the inner 3-4 mm of the ring's edge, then let the wax dry for 20-30 minutes. Stir the glaze thoroughly. The glaze should reach a specific gravity of about 1.45; tested empirically, glaze dripped from a stirrer should run off in an even stream rather than dropping in heavy clots.

Hold the plate by the waxed foot ring with three fingers spread evenly. Tilt to one side and submerge into the glaze bucket; hold for two seconds; rotate as the plate is lifted clear. Drain excess glaze back into the bucket. Touch up the three finger marks with a loaded liner brush. Set the plate on a dry rack and leave to dry for several hours.
Glaze firing

Load the plate flat on a freshly kiln-washed shelf. Do not stack plates in the glaze firing; glaze runs at full melt and any plate sitting on another will fuse. Fire to cone 6 (1222°C) following the kiln manufacturer's standard cone 6 glaze schedule, with a slow climb through quartz inversion at 573°C and a 10-15 minute hold at peak. Cool inside the kiln with the lid closed until the temperature reads 80°C or below before opening.
What it looks like when it is right

A finished plate sits flat on the bench without rocking; the foot ring contacts the surface evenly all the way round. The glaze surface is even with no bare spots and no runs. The rim shows a slightly thicker glaze line at the edge, which is normal. The plate rings when tapped lightly with a fingernail; a dull thud usually signals an incomplete glaze firing.
Variations

Speckled stoneware gives a more rustic finish; the iron specks burst through a transparent glaze as dark flecks. Porcelain produces a finer plate with a translucent rim, but porcelain plates warp more readily than stoneware and need slower drying. A bread-and-butter plate at 180 mm diameter takes 600 g of clay and follows the same sequence at smaller scale.

items.symptom: Floor cracks during drying (typically an S-shaped crack across the centre)
items.cause: Floor not compressed sufficiently after opening, or dried too fast
items.fix: Compress twice with a wooden rib at the wet stage; cover the rim with plastic during the first 24 hours of drying to slow the edge and let the centre catch up

items.symptom: Plate warps in the bisque firing (rim lifts off the shelf)
items.cause: Uneven wall thickness or rapid temperature rise through 500-700°C
items.fix: Compress floor and rim evenly during throwing; use a slow climb schedule with extra time between 200-700°C during bisque firing

items.symptom: Glaze runs onto the kiln shelf
items.cause: Glaze applied too thick at the foot ring area, or the wax did not cover the ring fully
items.fix: Apply wax to the entire foot ring and 3-4 mm up the inside and outside edges; touch up any glaze drips with a damp sponge before firing

items.symptom: Glaze crawls off the plate in patches
items.cause: Dust or finger grease on the bisque before dipping
items.fix: Wipe bisqueware with a damp sponge before glazing; handle by the foot ring only, never the rim or the upper surface

items.cause: Sharp edges on the trimmed ring; chipped during loading or handling
items.fix: Bevel both edges of the foot ring with a loop tool at the leather-hard stage; handle bisque plates by the underside, not the rim
